3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/RelativeSpec.lean
4	
5	% STRATEGY NOTE (iter-172). This chapter is the foundational A.1.a sub-row of
6	% Route A (positive-genus arm of nonempty_jacobianWitness). It packages the
7	% relative-spectrum functor \underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A}) : \mathrm{QcohAlg}(X)^{op}
8	% \to \mathrm{Sch}/X used by the relative Picard functor on a product C \times_k T.
9	% Mathlib has the absolute Spec functor and affine gluing (Scheme.GlueData,
10	% AffineScheme.glueOpens) but no general RelativeSpec construction; this chapter is
11	% the prover-ready specification for the new file AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/RelativeSpec.lean
12	% which iter-173 (file-skeleton lane) will scaffold.
13	
14	\section{Setup and motivation}
15	\label{sec:relspec_setup}
16	
17	The Route~A construction of $J = \Pic^0_{C/k}$ as a $g$-dimensional abelian variety is
18	organised around the relative Picard functor
19	$\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T) = \Pic(C \times_k T) / \pi_T^* \Pic(T)$
20	on the category of $k$-schemes. Representing $\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}$ requires the line-bundle
21	pullback construction on a relative curve $C \times_k T \to T$, which in turn rests on a
22	\emph{relative scheme} construction on a quasi-coherent sheaf of $\mathcal{O}_X$-algebras
23	$\mathcal{A}$, the \emph{relative spectrum}
24	$\pi : \underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A}) \to X$. This chapter packages the construction
25	of $\underline{\Spec}_X(-)$ and its three core properties --- universal property, affine
26	base, base change --- as the foundational A.1.a building block. The relative Picard
27	functor itself (A.1.c), the line-bundle pullback functor on a product (A.1.b), and the
28	identity-component / dimension theory of $J$ (A.3) live in separate sibling chapters
29	(\S~``Out of scope'' below).
30	
31	% SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Section ``Relative spectrum via glueing'' and ``Relative
32	% spectrum as a functor'' (tags 01LL, 01LO, 01LQ, 01LR, 01LS, available locally at
33	% references/stacks-constructions.tex L309-L720). The Hartshorne reference is
34	% [Hartshorne], II Exercise 5.17 (read from references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf).
35	
36	\section{Quasi-coherent sheaves of algebras}
37	\label{sec:qc_sheaf_of_algebras}
38	
39	\begin{definition}\leanok
40	[Quasi-coherent $\mathcal{O}_X$-algebra]
41	  \label{def:qc_sheaf_of_algebras}
42	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.QcohAlgebra}
43	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Section 01LL ``Relative spectrum via glueing'',
44	  % Situation ``situation-relative-spec''
45	  % (read from references/stacks-constructions.tex, L312-L318).
46	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
47	  % "Here $S$ is a scheme, and $\mathcal{A}$ is a quasi-coherent
48	  %  $\mathcal{O}_S$-algebra. This means that $\mathcal{A}$ is a
49	  %  sheaf of $\mathcal{O}_S$-algebras which is quasi-coherent as an
50	  %  $\mathcal{O}_S$-module."
51	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 01LL (situation-relative-spec).}
52	  Let $X$ be a scheme. A \emph{quasi-coherent sheaf of $\mathcal{O}_X$-algebras} is a
53	  sheaf $\mathcal{A}$ of $\mathcal{O}_X$-algebras (i.e. an $\mathcal{O}_X$-module
54	  equipped with $\mathcal{O}_X$-linear multiplication and unit morphisms satisfying
55	  associativity, commutativity, and the unit law as sheaf morphisms) whose underlying
56	  $\mathcal{O}_X$-module is quasi-coherent. We write $\mathrm{QcohAlg}(X)$ for the
57	  category of quasi-coherent sheaves of $\mathcal{O}_X$-algebras and
58	  $\mathcal{O}_X$-algebra morphisms.
59	\end{definition}
60	
61	% NOTE (iter-179 review): supersedes the iter-174 carrier note. The iter-179
62	% Block A refactor (analogies/relative-spec-encoding.md) upgrades the carrier
63	% to a 3-field structure
64	%     `structure QcohAlgebra (X : Scheme.{u}) where`
65	%     `  sheaf        : TopCat.Sheaf CommRingCat.{u} X.toPresheafedSpace`
66	%     `  unit         : X.sheaf ⟶ sheaf`
67	%     `  coequifibered : NatTrans.Coequifibered`
68	%     `    (Functor.whiskerLeft (AffineZariskiSite.toOpensFunctor X).op unit.hom)`
69	% The new third field IS the iter-175+ overlay the prior note flagged: Mathlib
70	% commit `b80f227`'s Stacks-01LL `NatTrans.Coequifibered` predicate is strictly
71	% weaker than the full `SheafOfModules.IsQuasicoherent` claimed by Definition
72	% 1.1's prose but is equivalent for the geometric purpose (constructing the
73	% relative-spectrum scheme) via the dense-subsite equivalence
74	% `AffineZariskiSite.sheafEquiv`. This is the predicate Mathlib's
75	% `AffineZariskiSite.relativeGluingData` consumes, so the iter-179
76	% `RelativeSpec`/`structureMorphism` bodies (now real Mathlib values) close
77	% against it.
78	
79	\section{The relative-spectrum scheme}
80	\label{sec:relspec_scheme}
81	
82	The construction is given affine-locally: on every affine open $U = \Spec R \subseteq X$ one
83	sets $\pi^{-1}(U) := \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U))$ regarded as an $R$-algebra, and the local pieces
84	glue compatibly because $\mathcal{A}$ is quasi-coherent (the transition isomorphism
85	$\mathcal{A}(U) \otimes_R S \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{A}(V)$ for an inclusion
86	$V = \Spec S \subseteq U$ gives an open immersion of the corresponding Specs).
87	
88	\begin{theorem}\leanok
89	[Relative spectrum exists]
90	  \label{thm:relative_spec_exists}
91	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RelativeSpec}
92	  \uses{def:qc_sheaf_of_algebras}
93	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], tag 01LQ (lemma-glue-relative-spec)
94	  % (read from references/stacks-constructions.tex, L381-L405).
95	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
96	  % "In Situation \ref{situation-relative-spec}.
97	  %  There exists a morphism of schemes
98	  %  $$
99	  %  \pi : \underline{\Spec}_S(\mathcal{A}) \longrightarrow S
100	  %  $$
101	  %  with the following properties:
102	  %  \begin{enumerate}
103	  %  \item for every affine open $U \subset S$ there exists an isomorphism
104	  %  $i_U : \pi^{-1}(U) \to \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U))$ over $U$, and
105	  %  \item for $U \subset U' \subset S$ affine open the composition
106	  %  $$
107	  %  \xymatrix{
108	  %  \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U)) \ar[r]^{i_U^{-1}} &
109	  %  \pi^{-1}(U) \ar[rr]^{inclusion} & &
110	  %  \pi^{-1}(U') \ar[r]^{i_{U'}} &
111	  %  \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U'))
112	  %  }
113	  %  $$
114	  %  is the open immersion of Lemma \ref{lemma-spec-inclusion} above.
115	  %  \end{enumerate}
116	  %  Moreover, $\underline{\Spec}_S(\mathcal{A})$
117	  %  is unique up to unique isomorphism over $S$."
118	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 01LQ (lemma-glue-relative-spec);
119	  cf.\ [Hartshorne], II~Ex.~5.17(a).}
120	  Let $X$ be a scheme and $\mathcal{A}$ a quasi-coherent $\mathcal{O}_X$-algebra
121	  (Definition~\ref{def:qc_sheaf_of_algebras}). There exists a scheme
122	  $\underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A})$ together with an affine morphism
123	  $\pi : \underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A}) \to X$ such that
124	  \begin{enumerate}
125	    \item for every affine open $U \subseteq X$, there is an isomorphism
126	      $i_U : \pi^{-1}(U) \xrightarrow{\sim} \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U))$ over $U$;
127	    \item for $U \subseteq U' \subseteq X$ both affine open, the composite
128	      $\Spec(\mathcal{A}(U)) \xrightarrow{i_U^{-1}} \pi^{-1}(U) \hookrightarrow
129	      \pi^{-1}(U') \xrightarrow{i_{U'}} \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U'))$
130	      coincides with the open immersion induced by the restriction
131	      $\mathcal{A}(U') \to \mathcal{A}(U)$.
132	  \end{enumerate}
133	  The pair $(\underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A}), \pi)$ is unique up to unique isomorphism
134	  over $X$.
135	\end{theorem}

137	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Follows immediately from
138	% Lemmas \ref{lemma-relative-glueing},
139	% \ref{lemma-spec-inclusion}, and
140	% \ref{lemma-transitive-spec}.
141	% Uniqueness is stated in the last sentence of
142	% Lemma \ref{lemma-relative-glueing}."
143	\begin{proof}
144	  \uses{def:qc_sheaf_of_algebras}
145	  By the open-cover gluing principle for schemes (Mathlib
146	  \texttt{Scheme.GlueData} / \texttt{AffineScheme.glueOpens}) it suffices to specify
147	  affine local pieces $\Spec(\mathcal{A}(U))$ for each affine open $U \subseteq X$ and
148	  open-immersion transition data
149	  $\Spec(\mathcal{A}(U)) \hookrightarrow \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U'))$ for $U \subseteq U'$
150	  satisfying the cocycle condition. The transition morphisms come from the ring maps
151	  $\mathcal{A}(U') \to \mathcal{A}(U)$; quasi-coherence yields the cartesian square
152	  $\Spec(\mathcal{A}(U)) = U \times_{U'} \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U'))$, so each transition is an
153	  open immersion. Transitivity (Stacks lemma-transitive-spec) gives the cocycle condition.
154	  Uniqueness of the glued scheme is the standard property of the gluing functor.
155	\end{proof}

229	  % iter-180+). Consumers may now rely on the affineness conclusion, but the
230	  % Yoneda-bijection witness is still deferred.
231	  Following Stacks tag 01LQ (proof of lemma-spec): the functor $F$ is a Zariski sheaf
232	  because morphisms of schemes and $\mathcal{O}$-algebra maps both glue under open covers.
233	  Choose an affine open cover $X = \bigcup U_i$. The subfunctor
234	  $F_i \subseteq F$ of pairs $(f, \varphi)$ with $f(T) \subseteq U_i$ is representable by
235	  $\Spec(\mathcal{A}(U_i))$ via the affine-base case
236	  (Theorem~\ref{thm:relative_spec_affine_base}) together with the
237	  base-change identification $F_i \cong F_{U_i,\,\mathcal{A}|_{U_i}}$

257	  % is available.

259	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], tag 01LO (lemma-spec-affine)
260	  % (read from references/stacks-constructions.tex, L491-L545).
261	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
262	  % "In Situation \ref{situation-relative-spec}.
263	  %  Let $F$ be the functor associated to $(S, \mathcal{A})$ above.
264	  %  If $S$ is affine, then $F$ is representable by the

267	  Let $X = \Spec R$ be an affine scheme and let $\mathcal{A}$ be a quasi-coherent
268	  $\mathcal{O}_X$-algebra. Set $A := \Gamma(X, \mathcal{A})$, regarded as an
269	  $R$-algebra. Then there is a canonical isomorphism of $X$-schemes
270	  \[

272	  \]

274	  spectrum of the global sections.

292	% \longrightarrow
293	% \widetilde A = \mathcal{O}_{\Spec(A)}
294	% $$
295	% coming from the $A$-module map $A \otimes_R A \to A$,

300	  % NOTE (iter-179 review): supersedes the iter-176 placeholder-discharge note.

306	  % commitment; still pending iter-180+).

308	  $\mathcal{A}$ gives $\mathcal{A} = \widetilde{A}$ as $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules
309	  (with the $R$-algebra structure on $A$ inducing the algebra structure on

370	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "A pair $(f' : T \to S', \varphi' : (f')^*\mathcal{A}' \to \mathcal{O}_T)$
371	% is the same as a pair $(f, \varphi : f^*\mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{O}_T)$
372	% together with a factorization of $f$ as $f = g \circ f'$. Namely with
373	% this notation we have

380	  % helpers ``Scheme.QcohAlgebra.pullback g 𝒜`` and
381	  % ``Scheme.RelativeSpec.pullback_iso g 𝒜`` (both substantive but with
382	  % ``sorry`` on Mathlib-gap content: the ``coequifibered`` field of the

390	  $S \to T \times_X \underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A})$ over $T$ is the same as a pair of an

401	\label{sec:relspec_functoriality}
402	
403	\begin{theorem}\leanok
404	[Relative spectrum as a functor]
405	  \label{thm:relative_spec_functorial}
406	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RelativeSpec.functor}
407	  \uses{def:qc_sheaf_of_algebras, thm:relative_spec_exists, thm:relative_spec_univ}
408	  % NOTE (iter-173 review): the iter-173 file-skeleton encodes this as a
409	  % bare object-level function ``QcohAlgebra → Over X'' (not a categorical
410	  % ``Functor''), with morphism action + functoriality + the equivalence
411	  % onto affine X-schemes all deferred. Iter-174+ upgrades to the full
412	  % contravariant ``Functor (X.QcohAlgebra)ᵒᵖ (Over X)'' with the universal
413	  % property-induced ``map'' once QcohAlgebra is unpacked.
414	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], tag 01LR (definition-relative-spec +
415	  % lemma-glueing-gives-functor-spec + lemma-spec-properties part (3))
416	  % (read from references/stacks-constructions.tex, L602-L691).
417	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
418	  % "Let $S$ be a scheme. Let $\mathcal{A}$ be a quasi-coherent sheaf of
419	  %  $\mathcal{O}_S$-algebras. The {\it relative spectrum of $\mathcal{A}$ over
420	  %  $S$}, or simply the {\it spectrum of $\mathcal{A}$ over $S$} is the scheme
421	  %  constructed in Lemma \ref{lemma-glue-relative-spec} which represents the
422	  %  functor $F$ (\ref{equation-spec}), see
423	  %  Lemma \ref{lemma-glueing-gives-functor-spec}.
424	  %  We denote it $\pi : \underline{\Spec}_S(\mathcal{A}) \to S$.
425	  %  The ``universal family'' is a morphism of $\mathcal{O}_S$-algebras
426	  %  $$
427	  %  \mathcal{A}
428	  %  \longrightarrow
429	  %  \pi_*\mathcal{O}_{\underline{\Spec}_S(\mathcal{A})}
430	  %  $$
431	  %  [...]
432	  %  The universal map
433	  %  $$
434	  %  \mathcal{A}
435	  %  \longrightarrow
436	  %  \pi_*\mathcal{O}_{\underline{\Spec}_S(\mathcal{A})}
437	  %  $$
438	  %  is an isomorphism of $\mathcal{O}_S$-algebras."
439	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 01LR (definition-relative-spec +
440	  lemma-glueing-gives-functor-spec + lemma-spec-properties);
441	  [Hartshorne], II~Ex.~5.17(b).}
442	  The construction $\mathcal{A} \mapsto \underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A})$ assembles into
443	  a contravariant functor
444	  \[
445	    \underline{\Spec}_X : \mathrm{QcohAlg}(X)^{op} \longrightarrow \mathrm{AffSch}/X
446	  \]
447	  from quasi-coherent $\mathcal{O}_X$-algebras to affine $X$-schemes: a morphism of
448	  $\mathcal{O}_X$-algebras $\psi : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}$ induces, by the universal
449	  property (Theorem~\ref{thm:relative_spec_univ}) applied to the pair
450	  $(\pi_\mathcal{B}, \psi \circ \pi_\mathcal{B}^*) :
451	  \pi_\mathcal{B}^*\mathcal{A} \to \pi_\mathcal{B}^*\mathcal{B} \to \mathcal{O}_{\Spec\mathcal{B}}$,
452	  an $X$-morphism
453	  $\underline{\Spec}_X(\psi) : \underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{B}) \to \underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A})$.
454	  Functoriality (identity, composition) is direct from the universal property. The
455	  pushforward $\pi_* \mathcal{O}_{\underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A})} \cong \mathcal{A}$
456	  exhibits $\underline{\Spec}_X$ as an equivalence between $\mathrm{QcohAlg}(X)^{op}$ and
457	  the full subcategory of affine $X$-schemes, with inverse $f \mapsto f_* \mathcal{O}_Y$
458	  (Stacks lemma-spec-properties part~(3)).
459	\end{theorem}
460	
461	\begin{proof}
462	  \uses{thm:relative_spec_exists, thm:relative_spec_univ, thm:relative_spec_affine_base}
463	  Functoriality follows directly from Theorem~\ref{thm:relative_spec_univ}: for
464	  $\psi : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}$, the pair
465	  $(\pi_\mathcal{B} : \underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{B}) \to X, \;
466	  \pi_\mathcal{B}^*(\psi))$ defines an $X$-morphism
467	  $\underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{B}) \to \underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A})$ via the
468	  representing universal pair. Identity and composition follow from naturality of the
469	  representing bijection in $\mathcal{A}$. For the equivalence claim, $\underline{\Spec}_X$
470	  is fully faithful because the isomorphism
471	  $\pi_* \mathcal{O}_{\underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A})} \cong \mathcal{A}$
472	  (locally identifying with $\Gamma(\Spec(\mathcal{A}(U)),\mathcal{O}) = \mathcal{A}(U)$
473	  via Theorem~\ref{thm:relative_spec_affine_base}) reconstructs $\mathcal{A}$ from
474	  $\underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A})$. Essential surjectivity onto affine $X$-schemes uses
475	  the canonical morphism $X \to \underline{\Spec}_X(\pi_*\mathcal{O}_X)$ of Stacks
476	  lemma-canonical-morphism; on an affine $X$-scheme $\pi : Y \to X$, this canonical
477	  morphism is an isomorphism (affine-locally it is the identity
478	  $\Spec\Gamma(\pi^{-1}(U), \mathcal{O}_Y) \to \Spec\Gamma(\pi^{-1}(U), \mathcal{O}_Y)$).
479	\end{proof}

481	\section{Lean encoding}
482	\label{sec:relspec_lean_encoding}
483	
484	The Lean target file is the new \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/RelativeSpec.lean}.
485	The construction proceeds by gluing absolute affine pieces: for each affine open
486	$U \subseteq X$ with $U = \Spec R$ and $A := \mathcal{A}(U)$ regarded as an
487	$R$-algebra, the local piece is $\Spec A$ with structure morphism $\Spec A \to \Spec R = U$
488	induced by the algebra unit $R \to A$. Mathlib provides \texttt{IsAffineOpen.toScheme} for
489	the affine-open inclusion and the absolute \texttt{Scheme.Spec} functor for the local
490	pieces; the transition isomorphisms come from quasi-coherence of $\mathcal{A}$ via
491	\texttt{QuasiCoherent.tensorIso} (or the explicit
492	$\mathcal{A}(U) \otimes_R S \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{A}(V)$ for $V = \Spec S \subseteq U$).
493	The gluing backbone is \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.GlueData} (general construction) or
494	the convenience wrapper \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.AffineScheme.glueOpens}; the cocycle
495	condition reduces to Stacks \texttt{lemma-transitive-spec}. The universal property
496	(Theorem~\ref{thm:relative_spec_univ}) is expressed as a
497	\texttt{CategoryTheory.Functor.RepresentableBy} witness, providing the natural bijection
498	$\Hom_X(T, \underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A})) \cong
499	\Hom_{\mathcal{O}_X\text{-alg}}(\mathcal{A}, g_*\mathcal{O}_T)$ as a
500	\texttt{Yoneda}-style structure. The base-change isomorphism
501	(Theorem~\ref{thm:relative_spec_base_change}) and functoriality
502	(Theorem~\ref{thm:relative_spec_functorial}) are derived from the universal property by
503	Yoneda. No new core axiom is required; the entire chapter is a definitional/structural
504	construction on top of existing Mathlib affine-scheme infrastructure.
505	
506	\section{Out of scope}
507	\label{sec:relspec_out_of_scope}
508	
509	This chapter packages \emph{only} the relative-spectrum construction and its three core
510	properties. The following downstream constructions live in separate sibling chapters and
511	are explicitly out of scope here:
512	\begin{itemize}
513	  \item \textbf{Relative Picard functor $\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}$} (A.1.c) --- consumed-by, not
514	    defined-here; its blueprint home is the planned chapter
515	    \texttt{Picard\_RelPicFunctor.tex}.
516	  \item \textbf{Line-bundle pullback on a product $C \times_k T$} (A.1.b) ---
517	    \texttt{Picard\_LineBundlePullback.tex} (planned).
518	  \item \textbf{Connectedness, dimensionality, smoothness of $\underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A})$}
519	    --- downstream properties that depend on assumptions on $\mathcal{A}$
520	    (locally finite type, flat, etc.), not unconditional consequences of the construction.
521	  \item \textbf{Identity-component $\Pic^0$ and abelian-variety structure} (A.3) --- a
522	    separate Route~A sub-row, fed by but not constructed in this chapter.
523	\end{itemize}

11	/-!
12	
13	# Equifibered natural transformation
14	
15	## Main definition
16	- `CategoryTheory.NatTrans.Equifibered`:
17	  A natural transformation `α : F ⟶ G` is equifibered if every commutative square of the following
18	  form is a pullback.
19	  ```
20	  F(X) → F(Y)
21	   ↓      ↓
22	  G(X) → G(Y)
23	  ```
24	- `CategoryTheory.NatTrans.Coequifibered`: The dual notion.
25	
26	-/
27	
28	@[expose] public section
29	
30	
31	open CategoryTheory.Limits CategoryTheory.Functor
32	
33	namespace CategoryTheory
34	
35	variable {J K C D ι : Type*} [Category* J] [Category* C] [Category* K] [Category* D]
36	
37	namespace NatTrans

39	/-- A natural transformation is equifibered if every commutative square of the following form is
40	a pullback.
41	```
42	F(X) → F(Y)
43	 ↓      ↓
44	G(X) → G(Y)
45	```
46	-/
47	def Equifibered : MorphismProperty (J ⥤ C) :=
48	  fun {F G} α ↦ ∀ ⦃i j : J⦄ (f : i ⟶ j), IsPullback (F.map f) (α.app i) (α.app j) (G.map f)
49	
50	theorem Equifibered.of_isIso {F G : J ⥤ C} (α : F ⟶ G) [IsIso α] : Equifibered α :=
51	  fun _ _ f => IsPullback.of_vert_isIso ⟨naturality _ f⟩
52	
53	@[deprecated (since := "2026-02-01")] alias equifibered_of_isIso := Equifibered.of_isIso
54	
55	theorem Equifibered.comp {F G H : J ⥤ C} {α : F ⟶ G} {β : G ⟶ H} (hα : Equifibered α)
56	    (hβ : Equifibered β) : Equifibered (α ≫ β) :=
57	  fun _ _ f => (hα f).paste_vert (hβ f)
58	
59	instance : (Equifibered (J := J) (C := C)).IsMultiplicative where
60	  id_mem _ := .of_isIso _
61	  comp_mem _ _ := .comp
62	
63	instance : (Equifibered (J := J) (C := C)).RespectsIso :=
64	  MorphismProperty.respectsIso_of_isStableUnderComposition fun _ _ ↦ .of_isIso
65	
66	theorem Equifibered.whiskerRight {F G : J ⥤ C} {α : F ⟶ G} (hα : Equifibered α)
67	    (H : C ⥤ D) [∀ (i j : J) (f : j ⟶ i), PreservesLimit (cospan (α.app i) (G.map f)) H] :
68	    Equifibered (whiskerRight α H) :=
69	  fun _ _ f => (hα f).map H

    (𝒜 : X.QcohAlgebra) :

/-- **Base change of a quasi-coherent algebra (pushforward formulation).**

Given a morphism `g : T ⟶ X` and a quasi-coherent `O_X`-algebra `𝒜`, the
pulled-back qcoh algebra `g^* 𝒜 : T.QcohAlgebra` is realised as the
pushforward of the structure sheaf of the relative-spec pullback. The
`sheaf` and `unit` fields are concrete (the topological pushforward of the
pullback's structure sheaf and the canonical natural transformation `q.c`
respectively, where `q := pullback.fst g (structureMorphism 𝒜)`). The
`coequifibered` field is supplied by the named helper
`QcohAlgebra.pullback_coequifibered`, which packages the Stacks-01LR
pushforward-along-affine-morphism compatibility. -/
noncomputable def QcohAlgebra.pullback {X T : Scheme.{u}} (g : T ⟶ X)
    (𝒜 : X.QcohAlgebra) : T.QcohAlgebra :=
  let q : CategoryTheory.Limits.pullback g (RelativeSpec.structureMorphism 𝒜) ⟶ T :=
    CategoryTheory.Limits.pullback.fst g (RelativeSpec.structureMorphism 𝒜)
  { sheaf := (TopCat.Sheaf.pushforward CommRingCat.{u} q.base).obj
      (CategoryTheory.Limits.pullback g (RelativeSpec.structureMorphism 𝒜)).sheaf
    unit := ⟨q.c⟩
    coequifibered := QcohAlgebra.pullback_coequifibered g 𝒜 }

namespace RelativeSpec

14	/-!
15	# Relative Normalization
16	
17	Given a qcqs morphism `f : X ⟶ Y`, we define the relative normalization `f.normalization`,
18	along with the maps that `f` factor into:
19	- `f.toNormalization : X ⟶ f.normalization`: a dominant morphism
20	- `f.fromNormalization : f.normalization ⟶ Y`: an integral morphism
21	
22	It satisfies the universal property:
23	For any factorization `X ⟶ T ⟶ Y` with `T ⟶ Y` integral,
24	the map `X ⟶ T` factors through `f.normalization` uniquely.
25	The factorization map is `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Hom.normalizationDesc`, and the uniqueness result
26	is `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Hom.normalization.hom_ext`.
27	
28	We also show that normalization commutes with disjoint unions and smooth base change.
29	
30	-/
31	
32	@[expose] public noncomputable section
33	
34	open CategoryTheory Limits
35	
36	namespace AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Hom
37	
38	universe u
39	
40	variable {X Y : Scheme.{u}} (f : X ⟶ Y)
41	
42	open AffineZariskiSite
43	
44	set_option backward.isDefEq.respectTransparency false in
45	/-- Given a morphism `f : X ⟶ Y`, this is the presheaf of integral closure of `Y` in `X`. -/
46	def normalizationDiagram : Y.Opensᵒᵖ ⥤ CommRingCat where
47	  obj U :=
48	    letI := (f.app U.unop).hom.toAlgebra
49	    .of (integralClosure Γ(Y, U.unop) Γ(X, f ⁻¹ᵁ U.unop))
50	  map {V U} i :=
51	    CommRingCat.ofHom ((X.presheaf.map (homOfLE (f.preimage_mono i.unop.le)).op).hom.restrict
52	      _ _ fun x hx ↦ by
53	      obtain ⟨U, rfl⟩ := Opposite.op_surjective U
54	      obtain ⟨V, rfl⟩ := Opposite.op_surjective V
55	      algebraize [(f.app U).hom, (f.app V).hom, (Y.presheaf.map i).hom,
56	        (X.presheaf.map (homOfLE (f.preimage_mono i.unop.le)).op).hom,
57	        (f.appLE V (f ⁻¹ᵁ U) (f.preimage_mono i.unop.le)).hom]
58	      have : IsScalarTower Γ(Y, V) Γ(Y, U) Γ(X, f ⁻¹ᵁ U) := .of_algebraMap_eq' <| by
59	        simp [RingHom.algebraMap_toAlgebra, ← CommRingCat.hom_comp]; rfl
60	      have : IsScalarTower Γ(Y, V) Γ(X, f ⁻¹ᵁ V) Γ(X, f ⁻¹ᵁ U) := .of_algebraMap_eq' rfl
61	      exact (hx.map (IsScalarTower.toAlgHom Γ(Y, V) _ Γ(X, f ⁻¹ᵁ U))).tower_top)
62	  map_id U := by simp; rfl
63	  map_comp i j := by
64	    simp only [← CommRingCat.ofHom_comp]
65	    rw [← homOfLE_comp (f.preimage_mono j.unop.le) (f.preimage_mono i.unop.le), op_comp]
66	    simp_rw [X.presheaf.map_comp]
67	    rfl
68	
69	/-- The inclusion from the structure presheaf of `Y` to the integral closure of `Y` in `X`. -/
70	def normalizationDiagramMap : Y.presheaf ⟶ f.normalizationDiagram where
71	  app U :=
72	    letI := (f.app U.unop).hom.toAlgebra
73	    CommRingCat.ofHom (algebraMap Γ(Y, U.unop) (integralClosure Γ(Y, U.unop) Γ(X, f ⁻¹ᵁ U.unop)))
74	  naturality {U V} i := by ext x; exact Subtype.ext congr($(f.naturality i) x)

81	  refine coequifibered_iff_forall_isLocalizationAway.mpr fun U r ↦ ?_

84	    (f.app (U.basicOpen r).1).hom.toAlgebra

87	    ((normalizationDiagram f).map (homOfLE (Y.basicOpen_le r)).op).hom.toAlgebra
88	  let inst : Algebra Γ(X, f ⁻¹ᵁ U.1) Γ(X, f ⁻¹ᵁ Y.basicOpen r) :=
89	    (X.presheaf.map (homOfLE (f.preimage_mono (Y.basicOpen_le r))).op).hom.toAlgebra
90	  have : IsLocalization.Away r Γ(Y, Y.basicOpen r) :=
91	    U.2.isLocalization_basicOpen _
92	  have : IsLocalization.Away ((algebraMap ↑Γ(Y, U.1) ↑Γ(X, f ⁻¹ᵁ U.1)) r)
93	      Γ(X, f ⁻¹ᵁ Y.basicOpen r) := by
94	    let : Algebra Γ(X, f ⁻¹ᵁ U.1) Γ(X, X.basicOpen (f.app _ r)) :=
95	      (X.presheaf.map (homOfLE (X.basicOpen_le _)).op).hom.toAlgebra
96	    dsimp +instances [inst]
97	    rw! (castMode := .all) [f.preimage_basicOpen r]
98	    exact isLocalization_basicOpen_of_qcqs (f.isCompact_preimage U.2.isCompact)
99	        (f.isQuasiSeparated_preimage U.2.isQuasiSeparated) (f.app _ r)
100	  change IsLocalization.Away ((algebraMap Γ(Y, U.1) (integralClosure Γ(Y, U.1) Γ(X, f ⁻¹ᵁ U.1))) r)
101	    (integralClosure Γ(Y, Y.basicOpen r) Γ(X, f ⁻¹ᵁ Y.basicOpen r))
102	  letI : Algebra ↑Γ(Y, U.1) ↑Γ(X, f ⁻¹ᵁ Y.basicOpen r) :=
103	    (f.appLE _ _ (f.preimage_mono (Y.basicOpen_le _))).hom.toAlgebra
104	  have : IsScalarTower Γ(Y, U.1) Γ(X, f ⁻¹ᵁ U.1) Γ(X, f ⁻¹ᵁ Y.basicOpen r) := .of_algebraMap_eq' rfl
105	  have : IsScalarTower Γ(Y, U.1) Γ(Y, Y.basicOpen r) Γ(X, f ⁻¹ᵁ Y.basicOpen r) :=
106	    .of_algebraMap_eq' <| by
107	      simp only [RingHom.algebraMap_toAlgebra, ← CommRingCat.hom_comp, Scheme.Hom.app_eq_appLE,
108	        Scheme.Hom.map_appLE, AffineZariskiSite.basicOpen]
109	  have : IsScalarTower (integralClosure Γ(Y, U.1) Γ(X, f ⁻¹ᵁ U.1))
110	    (integralClosure Γ(Y, Y.basicOpen r) Γ(X, f ⁻¹ᵁ Y.basicOpen r))
111	    Γ(X, f ⁻¹ᵁ Y.basicOpen r) := .of_algebraMap_eq' rfl
112	  have : IsScalarTower Γ(Y, U.1) (integralClosure Γ(Y, U.1) Γ(X, f ⁻¹ᵁ U.1))
113	    (integralClosure Γ(Y, Y.basicOpen r) Γ(X, f ⁻¹ᵁ Y.basicOpen r)) := .of_algebraMap_eq' rfl
114	  exact IsLocalization.Away.integralClosure r

389	theorem isLocalization_basicOpen_of_qcqs {X : Scheme} {U : X.Opens} (hU : IsCompact U.1)
390	    (hU' : IsQuasiSeparated U.1) (f : Γ(X, U)) :
391	    IsLocalization.Away f (Γ(X, X.basicOpen f)) := by
392	  constructor; constructor
393	  · rintro ⟨_, n, rfl⟩
394	    simp only [map_pow, RingHom.algebraMap_toAlgebra]
395	    exact IsUnit.pow _ (RingedSpace.isUnit_res_basicOpen _ f)
396	  · intro z
397	    obtain ⟨n, y, e⟩ := exists_eq_pow_mul_of_isCompact_of_isQuasiSeparated X U hU hU' f z
398	    refine ⟨⟨y, _, n, rfl⟩, ?_⟩
399	    simpa only [map_pow, Subtype.coe_mk, RingHom.algebraMap_toAlgebra, mul_comm z] using e.symm
400	  · intro x y
401	    rw [← sub_eq_zero, ← map_sub, RingHom.algebraMap_toAlgebra]
402	    simp_rw [← @sub_eq_zero _ _ (_ * x) (_ * y), ← mul_sub]
403	    generalize x - y = z
404	    intro H
405	    obtain ⟨n, e⟩ := exists_pow_mul_eq_zero_of_res_basicOpen_eq_zero_of_isCompact X hU _ _ H
406	    refine ⟨⟨_, n, rfl⟩, ?_⟩
407	    simpa [mul_comm z] using e

410	    (hU : IsCompact U.carrier) (hU' : IsQuasiSeparated U.carrier)
411	    {f g s : Γ(X, U)} (hfg : f |_ X.basicOpen s = g |_ X.basicOpen s) :
412	    ∃ n, s ^ n * f = s ^ n * g := by
413	  obtain ⟨n, hc⟩ := (isLocalization_basicOpen_of_qcqs hU hU' s).exists_of_eq s hfg

Explanation: The rewrite tactic rewrites an expression 'e' using an equality 'a = b' by the following process. First, it looks for all 'a' in 'e'. Second, it tries to abstract these occurrences of 'a' to create a function 'm := fun _a => ...', called the *motive*, with the property that 'm a' is definitionally equal to 'e'. Third, we observe that 'congrArg' implies that 'm a = m b', which can be used with lemmas such as 'Eq.mpr' to change the goal. However, if 'e' depends on specific properties of 'a', then the motive 'm' might not typecheck.

Possible solutions: use rewrite's 'occs' configuration option to limit which occurrences are rewritten, or use 'simp' or 'conv' mode, which have strategies for certain kinds of dependencies (these tactics can handle proofs and 'Decidable' instances whose types depend on the rewritten term, and 'simp' can apply user-defined '@[congr]' theorems as well).

105:/-- A `TopCat.Sheaf C X` is a presheaf of objects from `C` over a (bundled) topological space `X`,
128:def forget : TopCat.Sheaf C X ⥤ TopCat.Presheaf C X :=

100	
101	end Presheaf
102	
103	variable (C X)
104	
105	/-- A `TopCat.Sheaf C X` is a presheaf of objects from `C` over a (bundled) topological space `X`,
106	satisfying the sheaf condition.
107	-/
108	nonrec def Sheaf : Type max u v w :=
109	  Sheaf (Opens.grothendieckTopology X) C
110	deriving Category
111

115	abbrev Sheaf.presheaf (F : X.Sheaf C) : TopCat.Presheaf C X :=
116	  F.1
117

121	instance sheafInhabited : Inhabited (Sheaf (CategoryTheory.Discrete PUnit) X) :=

123	
124	namespace Sheaf
125	
126	/-- The forgetful functor from sheaves to presheaves.
127	-/
128	def forget : TopCat.Sheaf C X ⥤ TopCat.Presheaf C X :=
129	  sheafToPresheaf _ _

/-- **Stacks 01LL Coequifibered overlay for the pushforward of `O_P` along an
affine `q : P ⟶ T`** (iter-180 Lane C, Stacks 01LR pushforward compatibility).

Per the Mathlib characterization
`Scheme.AffineZariskiSite.coequifibered_iff_forall_isLocalizationAway`, the
claim reduces to: for every affine open `U ⊆ T` and section `r : Γ(T, U.1)`,
the basic-open restriction `q_* O_P (D(r)) = Γ(P, q⁻¹(D(r)))` is the
`IsLocalization.Away` of `q.app r` over `Γ(P, q⁻¹U)`. The named helper
`pullback_fst_isAffineHom` exhibits `q` as affine, so every preimage
`q⁻¹U` of an affine open `U` is itself affine; combined with
`Scheme.Hom.preimage_basicOpen` (which identifies `q⁻¹(T.basicOpen r)` with
`P.basicOpen (q.app U.1 r)`), `IsAffineOpen.isLocalization_of_eq_basicOpen`
supplies the localization with the desired algebra structure (restriction
along the inclusion `q⁻¹(U.basicOpen r) ⊆ q⁻¹U` induced by
`q.preimage_mono` on the AffineZariski-site inclusion). -/
lemma QcohAlgebra.pullback_coequifibered {X T : Scheme.{u}} (g : T ⟶ X)
    (𝒜 : X.QcohAlgebra) :
    NatTrans.Coequifibered ((AffineZariskiSite.toOpensFunctor T).op.whiskerLeft
      ({ hom := (CategoryTheory.Limits.pullback.fst g
            (RelativeSpec.structureMorphism 𝒜)).c } :
        T.sheaf ⟶ (TopCat.Sheaf.pushforward CommRingCat.{u}
          (CategoryTheory.Limits.pullback.fst g
            (RelativeSpec.structureMorphism 𝒜)).base).obj
          (CategoryTheory.Limits.pullback g
            (RelativeSpec.structureMorphism 𝒜)).sheaf).hom) := by
  haveI : IsAffineHom (CategoryTheory.Limits.pullback.fst g
      (RelativeSpec.structureMorphism 𝒜)) :=
    QcohAlgebra.pullback_fst_isAffineHom g 𝒜
  refine AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.AffineZariskiSite.coequifibered_iff_forall_isLocalizationAway.mpr
    fun U r => ?_
  set q := CategoryTheory.Limits.pullback.fst g (RelativeSpec.structureMorphism 𝒜)
  have hqU : IsAffineOpen (q ⁻¹ᵁ U.1) := U.2.preimage q
  have hle : (U.basicOpen r).toOpens ≤ U.toOpens := T.basicOpen_le r
  exact hqU.isLocalization_of_eq_basicOpen (q.app U.1 r)
    (homOfLE (q.preimage_mono hle)) (q.preimage_basicOpen r)

# AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/RelativeSpec.lean — Lane C iter-180

## Outcome: **SUCCESS** (axiom-clean closure of `coequifibered` field)

Lane C target: close the `coequifibered` field in `QcohAlgebra.pullback`.

**Result**: `coequifibered := QcohAlgebra.pullback_coequifibered g 𝒜` —
fully closed kernel-only via two named helpers. File compiles GREEN with
only the off-target `pullback_iso` sorry remaining (line 429).

## Sorry count

- **Before**: 2 sorries (`QcohAlgebra.pullback.coequifibered` L236, `pullback_iso` L353).
- **After**: 1 sorry (`pullback_iso` L429 — off-target this iter, iter-181+ work).
- Net: **-1 sorry**, **0 new axioms**.

## Helpers introduced (budget = 2, both used)

1. `Scheme.QcohAlgebra.pullback_fst_isAffineHom` (line ~309):
   `IsAffineHom (pullback.fst g (RelativeSpec.structureMorphism 𝒜))`
   via `RelativeSpec.UniversalProperty 𝒜` +
   `MorphismProperty.pullback_fst`. **Axiom-clean.**

2. `Scheme.QcohAlgebra.pullback_coequifibered` (line ~329):
   `NatTrans.Coequifibered ((toOpensFunctor T).op.whiskerLeft q.c)`
   via `coequifibered_iff_forall_isLocalizationAway.mpr` +
   `IsAffineOpen.isLocalization_of_eq_basicOpen` +
   `Scheme.Hom.preimage_basicOpen`. **Axiom-clean.**

`#print axioms` on both returns `{propext, Classical.choice, Quot.sound}`
only.

## QcohAlgebra.pullback coequifibered (line 236 → moved to L370)

### Attempt 1
- **Approach**: Reorder helpers to feed `coequifibered` directly via
  Mathlib's `coequifibered_iff_forall_isLocalizationAway`.
- **Result**: RESOLVED — closed axiom-clean.
- **Key insight**: After reducing to the per-`(U, r)` `IsLocalization.Away`
  goal via the iff lemma, the right Mathlib bridge is
  `IsAffineOpen.isLocalization_of_eq_basicOpen` (NOT
  `IsAffineOpen.isLocalization_basicOpen` directly). The `_of_eq_` variant
  lets us pass in the open `q ⁻¹ᵁ (U.basicOpen r).1` together with the
  equation `q ⁻¹ᵁ (U.basicOpen r).1 = P.basicOpen (q.app U.1 r)` (which
  is `Scheme.Hom.preimage_basicOpen`), avoiding the dependent-type
  unification problems that plague `convert` over the algebra instances.
  The algebra structure produced by `_of_eq_basicOpen` is
  `(X.presheaf.map i.op).hom.toAlgebra` with explicit `i : V ⟶ U`, which
  exactly matches the `letI` algebra structure the iff lemma's RHS uses
  (the pushforward-presheaf restriction map).

### Lemmas/idioms discovered/used
- `MorphismProperty.pullback_fst _ _ inferInstance` for affineness stability under base change.
- `IsAffineOpen.preimage` (on a `IsAffineHom` morphism) → affineness of affine-open preimages.
- `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.AffineZariskiSite.coequifibered_iff_forall_isLocalizationAway`
  — the iff reducing Coequifibered to a per-`(U, f)` `IsLocalization.Away`.
- `IsAffineOpen.isLocalization_of_eq_basicOpen` — variant of
  `isLocalization_basicOpen` accepting an explicit `V ⟶ U` and `V = X.basicOpen f`
  proof, sidestepping algebra-instance matching when `V` is not literally
  `X.basicOpen f` but proven equal to it.
- `Scheme.Hom.preimage_basicOpen` — `f ⁻¹ᵁ Y.basicOpen r = X.basicOpen (f.app U r)`.
- `Scheme.Hom.preimage_mono` — preimage is monotone in the open.
- `Scheme.basicOpen_le` — basic open is contained in the ambient open.

### Negative search results
- Tried `rw [coequifibered_iff_forall_isLocalizationAway]` directly: failed because
  the LHS pattern uses `(toLocallyRingedSpace X).presheaf` while the
  goal carries `T.sheaf.obj` (the pushforward sheaf). `refine ... .mpr fun U r => ?_`
  is the right move (no rewrite on a different-shaped iff).
- Tried `convert key using N` with `IsAffineOpen.isLocalization_basicOpen`:
  produces unwieldy `⋯ ▸ CommRing.toCommSemiring` cast goals where the
  algebra instance can't be unified without solving for HEq of
  `Algebra` instances (NOT subsingleton, so neither `Subsingleton.elim`
  nor `proof_irrel_heq` close).
- Tried `rw! (castMode := .all) [preimage_basicOpen ...]` followed by
  `exact isLocalization_basicOpen ...`: produces `⋯ ▸ Algebra` cast in the
  expected goal type, same dependent-type problem as above.

## pullback_iso (line 429) — leave sorry (off-target this iter)

Per Lane C directive: "The OTHER helper `pullback_iso` STAYS sorry this
iter (separate body, iter-181+ work)."

## Reorganization

`QcohAlgebra.pullback` def was moved from BEFORE `namespace RelativeSpec`
to AFTER `RelativeSpec.UniversalProperty` (closing the namespace and
re-opening), because the new helper `pullback_fst_isAffineHom` depends
on `RelativeSpec.UniversalProperty`. The orphan doc-comment from the
old position was converted to a `/-! ... -/` section comment (with
updated text describing the new placement). No signatures changed.

## Build status

- `lake env lean AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/RelativeSpec.lean`: only the
  `pullback_iso` (L429) sorry warning emitted; 0 errors.
- Three `#print axioms` checks (`pullback_fst_isAffineHom`,
  `pullback_coequifibered`, `pullback`) all return
  `{propext, Classical.choice, Quot.sound}` (kernel-only).

## Blueprint markers

The blueprint chapter (`chapters/Picard_RelativeSpec.tex`) carries
`\lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.QcohAlgebra}` etc. on the 6 pinned
declarations. The two new helpers
(`QcohAlgebra.pullback_fst_isAffineHom`,
`QcohAlgebra.pullback_coequifibered`) are unpinned auxiliary lemmas
feeding the `QcohAlgebra.pullback` constructor and are not blueprint
pins themselves. The deterministic `sync_leanok` will pick up the
`\leanok` changes (none here — file's pinned declarations didn't change
type signatures or sorry counts beyond the off-target `pullback_iso`).

## Suggested next step (iter-181+)

`pullback_iso` (L429) body: assemble the cocone over the directed
affine cover of `T` and invoke the colimit universal property of
`Cover.RelativeGluingData.glued`. Each affine open `U ⊆ T` has
preimage `q⁻¹U` affine (via the now-proven
`pullback_fst_isAffineHom`), and these affine pieces should be
Mathlib-isomorphic to `Spec((g^*𝒜).sheaf(U))` by construction of
`QcohAlgebra.pullback.sheaf` as the pushforward. The remaining work
is structural cocone assembly + colimit-descent.

Key technical move: used `IsAffineOpen.isLocalization_of_eq_basicOpen` (the variant accepting an explicit `V ⟶ U` plus `V = X.basicOpen f` equation) rather than `isLocalization_basicOpen` directly — sidestepped the `convert`/`rw!` dependent-type cast problems that arose when trying to match the iff lemma's `letI` algebra structure against `algebra_section_section_basicOpen`.

content:
1	# Archon Project
2	
3	You are one of: the plan agent, a prover agent, a subagent (per descriptor in `.archon/subagents/`), or the review agent. Read `PROGRESS.md` to determine your role and current objectives. Keep workspace tidy. Prefer existing MCP tools.
4	
5	## Priority Rule
6	
7	When instructions conflict between global and local sources, **local takes precedence**:
8	
9	- Prompts in `.archon/prompts/` override Archon's global prompts.
10	- Skills in `.claude/skills/` override globally installed plugins.
11	- Rules in `.claude/rules/` apply only to this project.
12	
13	## Skills
14	
15	- **archon-lean4** — installed as `lean4@archon-local` plugin (live-linked to Archon source). Provides `/archon-lean4:prove`, `/archon-lean4:golf`, `/archon-lean4:doctor`, etc.
16	
17	## Tools
18	
19	Project tools live in `.claude/tools/` as directly-executable scripts. List with `ls .claude/tools/`; run `<path> --help`. MCP servers (Lean LSP, etc.) are registered in `.claude/settings.json` / `.mcp.json` and surface as `mcp__*` tools.
20	
21	Two always-present scripts:
22	
23	- **`archon-informal-agent.py`** — call external LLMs (OpenAI/Gemini/OpenRouter) for an informal proof-style sketch when you're stuck on an approach and want a second opinion. **Requires `OPENAI_API_KEY` / `GEMINI_API_KEY` / `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` in env** — check via `env | grep -E "OPENAI|GEMINI|OPENROUTER"` BEFORE planning around its use; if no key is set, fall back to alternatives below. The output is LLM-generated from training data, NOT source-derived: this tool is **not** a literature retriever. For actual literature lookup (cross-check a claim against Hartshorne, fetch an arXiv paper, summarize a Stacks tag) consult the auto-injected subagent catalog — when a literature/reference-fetching subagent is enabled it will appear there — or use the `WebSearch` / `WebFetch` tools directly.
24	- **`archon-subagent.py`** — the generic subagent dispatcher (one wrapper handles every subagent — see "Subagents" below).
25	
26	## Subagents
27	
28	Subagents are descriptor files at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md` (YAML frontmatter — `name`, `description`, `write_domain`, `read_only`, `can_spawn`, `default_enabled`, optional `mandatory: [<phase>...]`, optional `dispatcher_notes` — followed by the prompt body the spawned Claude reads).
29	
30	**You do NOT need to discover subagents.** The plan and review prompts auto-inject an **Available subagents** section at the top of each invocation listing every enabled descriptor with description, write-domain, MANDATORY flag, and `dispatcher_notes`. When you decide to invoke a subagent, read its full prompt at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md`.
31	
32	**Invoke** via the generic wrapper (Bash tool, foreground):
33	
34	```
35	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
36	  --name <subagent-name> \
37	  --slug <kebab-slug> \
38	  --directive-file <path-to-directive.md> \
39	  --write-domain '<glob>'        # repeat for multiple
40	```
41	
42	The wrapper exits 0 on success, prints a one-line status, writes the report to `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`, and the Archon CLI auto-archives a copy to `logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-report.md` for the dashboard. The dispatch semaphore caps concurrent subagent processes at `loop.max_parallel`; the wrapper resolves `--parent-slug` from `ARCHON_SUBAGENT_SLUG` so nested children inherit the hierarchy.
43	
44	**Treat each dispatch as blocking — wait for its report before acting on it.** Don't *deliberately* set `run_in_background: true`. But note the wrapper is genuinely synchronous (it `subprocess.run`s the `archon subagent` CLI and only returns once the child finishes and its report is on disk), so the harness may *auto-background* a long-running dispatch and hand you a task ID immediately — that's expected and fine. When it does, await that task / poll for the report file at `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md` (the wrapper's one-line status and exit 0 confirm completion) before you read or act on the result. The thing to avoid is firing a dispatch and then continuing to plan as if it had already returned; whether the call blocked inline or was auto-backgrounded, the rule is the same: do not consume a subagent's output until its report exists.
45	
46	**Highly-recommended subagents.** A descriptor whose frontmatter sets `mandatory: [plan]` is treated as **highly recommended** for the plan phase (similarly `[review]` for the review phase). The catalog tags them `[HIGHLY RECOMMENDED]`. Dispatch each one unless you have a concrete reason to skip — when skipping, record a one-line rationale under a `## Subagent skips` section in `iter/iter-NNN/<phase>.md` (e.g. `- strategy-critic: STRATEGY.md SHA unchanged from prior iter and last verdict was SOUND`). A post-phase audit checks both signals: it silences when the subagent dispatched OR a skip rationale was recorded, and warns only when neither happened. Each subagent's `dispatcher_notes` enumerates its specific skip conditions — read them before deciding. **Subagents ship disabled by default** (`default_enabled: false`), so the rule only fires for subagents the user has explicitly listed under `subagents.enabled` in `.archon/config.json`; on a fresh project the catalog is empty and no recommended dispatch is required. (The frontmatter field is still named `mandatory: [...]` for backward compat with existing configs and descriptors — its semantic meaning is "highly recommended", not "must dispatch".)
47	
48	## Key Files & Permissions
49	
50	All Archon state files are in `.archon/`. The table below covers **phase agents** (plan, prover, review) and the **user**. Subagent permissions are NOT a fixed row — they are descriptor-driven (`write_domain` glob in the subagent's frontmatter, enforced at dispatch time). See "Subagent permissions" after the table.
51	
52	| File | Plan | Prover | Review | User |
53	|------|------|--------|--------|------|
54	| `PROGRESS.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
55	| `STRATEGY.md` | read + write | — | — | read |
56	| `USER_HINTS.md` | **loop-managed** (captured + cleared by the CLI; injected into the plan prompt) | — | — | write |
57	| `task_pending.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
58	| `task_done.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
59	| `task_results/<file>.md` | read (collect) | write (own file) | read only | read |
60	| `proof-journal/` | read | — | **write** | read |
61	| `PROJECT_STATUS.md` | read | — | **write** (Knowledge Base only — session log lives in `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`) | read |
62	| `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md` | **write** (this iter only) | — | read (recent window injected) | read |
63	| `iter/iter-NNN/review.md` | read (recent window injected) | — | **write** (this iter only) | read |
64	| `iter/iter-NNN/objectives.md` | optional write | — | read | read |
65	| `archon-protected.yaml` | read | read | read | **write** |
66	| `.lean` files | — | write (own file; frozen protected signatures) | — | write (via comments) |
67	| `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex` | **write** (informal prose, `\lean{...}` hints) | — | **write** (semantic markers: `\mathlibok`, `% NOTE:`, `\lean{...}` corrections — NOT `\leanok`) | read |
68	| `analogies/<slug>.md` | read (when relevant) | — | — | read |
69	| `TO_USER.md` | — (review owns) | — | **write** (reset at review start) | read |
70	
71	### Subagent permissions
72	
73	Each subagent ships YAML frontmatter:
74	
75	- `write_domain: "<glob>"` — file pattern the subagent may write. Empty for purely read-only subagents (the dispatching call typically passes `--write-domain 'task_results/**'` so the subagent can still write its own report).
76	- `read_only: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may only write its `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`.
77	- `can_spawn: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may dispatch children whose `write_domain` is a subset of its own.
78	
79	The dispatcher enforces `write_domain` at the filesystem level: any child whose declared domain is not a subset of the parent's is rejected before the child agent starts. Reads are restricted only by the descriptor's prompt body (e.g. fresh-context critics that are told not to read PROGRESS.md / STRATEGY.md).
80	
81	**`.archon/REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md`** is reserved for the interactive `archon refactor draft` / `archon refactor run` flow run by hand by the mathematician. The autonomous loop never reads or writes that file. To invoke a refactor-style subagent inside the loop, write the directive to a fresh tempfile under `.archon/logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-directive.md` and dispatch via the generic wrapper. Older state files (`STRATEGY.md`, `task_pending.md`, `PROGRESS.md`) may contain leftover references to the old REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md flow — treat those as historical noise.
82	
83	## Protected Declarations
84	
85	`archon-protected.yaml` at the project root lists declarations whose **signatures are frozen** by the mathematician. Rules:
86	
87	- **Plan / prover / review** agents: read-only on protected signatures. May fill proof bodies; may not rename, re-type, or reorder arguments.
88	- **Write-capable structural subagents** (per their `write_domain`): may *move* a protected declaration to a different file (name + signature preserved verbatim) and must then update the path key in `archon-protected.yaml`. They may NEVER rename / re-type / delete / re-sign a protected declaration.
89	- The YAML file itself is user-only. No agent adds or removes a declaration. The only allowed agent edit is updating an existing declaration's file path.
90	
91	## Blueprint Marker Vocabulary
92	
93	Two semantic markers:
94	
95	- `\leanok` — statement block: declaration is formalized (at least a `sorry` present). Proof block: proof closed, no `sorry`.
96	- `\mathlibok` — statement block: declaration is backed by Mathlib (re-export / alias); no Archon proof obligation remains.
97	
98	No marker means the block is unformalized. If a block fails to translate, leave it unmarked and annotate with a `% NOTE:` comment.
99	
100	**`\leanok` is managed deterministically by the `sync_leanok` phase** that runs between prover and review each iteration. It walks every chapter, looks up each `\lean{...}` declaration, runs `sorry_analyzer` + `lake env lean`, and adds/removes `\leanok` accordingly. Agents must NOT add or remove `\leanok` themselves.
101	
102	`\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, and `% NOTE:` annotations are the review agent's domain (they require semantic judgement). The plan agent writes informal prose and `\lean{...}` hints; provers never touch the blueprint.
103	
104	## User Interaction
105	
106	The user provides hints in two places:
107	
108	- **Strategic hints** → `.archon/USER_HINTS.md`. The Archon loop captures this file's content before each plan phase, injects it into the plan agent's prompt, and resets the file to its bundled template (an HTML-comment format guide + zero bullets) after the plan phase succeeds. The HTML-comment preamble is stripped before injection so a template-only file renders as "no hints" to the planner. The plan agent does NOT read or clear that file itself. Provers never see these hints.
109	- **File-specific hints** → `/- USER: ... -/` comments directly in `.lean` files. The prover that owns that file sees them naturally.
110	
111	## Agent Roles
112	
113	### Plan Agent
114	
115	- Read `.archon/prompts/plan.md` for full instructions.
116	- User hints, blueprint-doctor findings, recent iter sidecars, the subagent catalog, and the references summary are all pre-injected into your invocation prompt. You do not "go read" those files.
117	- Read `task_results/` to collect prover and subagent results, then update `task_pending.md` / `task_done.md`.
118	- Optionally invoke subagents (via the auto-injected catalog) before writing prover objectives. Subagent reports are auto-archived to `logs/iter-NNN/` by the Archon CLI.
119	- Write `PROGRESS.md` with objectives for the next prover round.
120	- Write informal prose in `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex`. Do NOT touch markers — `\leanok` is owned by the deterministic sync; `\mathlibok` / `% NOTE:` by the review agent.
121	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or fill sorries yourself.
122	
123	### Prover Agent
124	
125	- Read `PROGRESS.md` for your objectives (read only).
126	- Read the stage-specific prompt from `.archon/prompts/` (`prover-autoformalize.md` / `prover-prover.md` / `prover-polish.md`).
127	- Write results to `task_results/<your_file>.md`.
128	- Write only to the `.lean` file(s) you are assigned — never edit another agent's file.
129	- Check for `/- USER: ... -/` comments in your file for file-specific hints.
130	- Do NOT edit blueprint chapters. Flag in your task result which declarations are ready for which marker; the review agent applies them.
131	
132	### Subagents
133	
134	Subagents are descriptor-driven and discovered at runtime. The catalog is auto-injected into the plan and review prompts each phase (see "Subagents" above for the wrapper invocation). Each subagent reads only what its directive points at — never `PROGRESS.md` / `STRATEGY.md` / phase-agent state — and writes a self-contained report at `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`. Descriptors with `can_spawn: true` may dispatch children (subject to the global `loop.max_parallel` cap).
135	
136	### Review Agent
137	
138	- Read `.archon/prompts/review.md` for full instructions.
139	- Read `proof-journal/current_session/attempts_raw.jsonl` for structured prover attempt data.
140	- Write the session journal to `proof-journal/sessions/session_N/` (`summary.md`, `milestones.jsonl`, `recommendations.md`).
141	- Update `PROJECT_STATUS.md` (Knowledge Base only — session narrative goes to `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`).
142	- Maintain the semantic blueprint markers — `\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, `% NOTE:` annotations, stale `\notready` removal. Do NOT touch `\leanok`.
143	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or modify `PROGRESS.md`.
144	
145	### Loop infrastructure (no agent role)
146	
147	These run automatically between phases:
148	
149	- **Iter sidecar init** — creates `.archon/iter/iter-NNN/` at iter start so plan + review have a stable destination for per-iter narrative. Top-level files (STRATEGY.md, PROJECT_STATUS.md, task_*.md) stay bounded.
150	- **`sync_leanok`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically updating `\leanok` markers based on actual sorry counts and compilation status.
151	- **`blueprint-doctor`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically detecting orphan chapters, broken `\ref` / `\uses` / `\proves`, and new `axiom` declarations. Findings are written to `logs/iter-NNN/blueprint-doctor.{md,json}` and inlined into the next plan agent's prompt.
152

1	# Prover — Prover Stage
2	
3	You fill `sorry` placeholders with complete proofs in your assigned `.lean` file.
4	
5	## Workflow
6	
7	1. Read `PROGRESS.md` for your objectives (read only).
8	2. Read `task_pending.md` for prior attempts, dead ends, relevant lemmas on your file.
9	3. Check the `.lean` file for `/- USER: ... -/` comments (file-specific user hints).
10	4. **Read the relevant blueprint chapter before writing Lean code.** The chapter holds the mathematical proof sketch you must align with. When stuck, re-reading the blueprint is often the fastest path forward.
11	5. Replace `sorry` with Lean proofs. Push as far as possible.
12	6. **Always save partial progress in the code.** If you can't fully close a sorry, leave your best attempt — commented-out steps, helper lemmas, partial `by` blocks with `sorry` at the stuck point. The file must still compile (use scoped `sorry` if needed), but your work must be visible for the next agent to continue from. NEVER revert to a bare `sorry` — that wastes the work.
13	7. Write your results to `task_results/<your_file>.md`.
14	
15	**Write permissions**: only your assigned `.lean` file(s) and `task_results/<your_file>.md`. Do NOT edit `PROGRESS.md`, `task_pending.md`, `task_done.md`, blueprint chapters, or other agents' files.
16	
17	## Protected declarations
18	
19	`archon-protected.yaml` lists declarations with **frozen signatures**. You may fill their proof bodies but must not rename, re-type, reorder arguments, or weaken hypotheses. Only the mathematician edits protected signatures.
20	
21	## Avoid early termination
22	
23	- Don't abandon a proof prematurely. Many complex proofs run to thousands of lines.
24	- Difficulty is NOT a valid reason to leave a `sorry`.
25	- Don't delegate to "the next iteration" or "another prover" if more effort could close it.
26	- Only modify the proof for your assigned task — leave unrelated proofs untouched.
27	- **Decompose**: break the proof into smaller sub-problems (following the blueprint's lemma structure when available: L1, L2, L3, …) and solve each individually until the entire goal closes.
28	
29	## Task completion criteria
30	
31	Your task is complete ONLY when ALL of:
32	
33	1. Every `sorry` in scope is replaced with a complete proof.
34	2. Zero axioms introduced.
35	3. The file compiles cleanly.
36	
37	## Never weaken the type to dodge the proof
38	
39	When the substantive type is unattainable this iter, you have one honest move: leave `sorry` with the **intended type signature** so the type itself encodes the claim. You must NOT substitute a tautologically-true type to trivialize the body. Three patterns are banned:
40	
41	- **Reflexive-iso placeholder.** Replacing a non-trivial `X ≅ Y` (or `Nonempty (X ≅ Y)`) target with `Nonempty (X ≅ X) := ⟨Iso.refl _⟩`. The type now asserts nothing; the docstring's "iter-N placeholder" disclosure does not undo the structural lie, and the blueprint's `\lean{...}` cross-reference will resolve to this hollow declaration.
42	- **`Classical.choice` body around an explicit witness.** Writing `Classical.choice (α := X) ⟨witness⟩` to dissolve `Type` vs `Prop` friction — the witness is unreachable through any unfold, so the def is indistinguishable from `Classical.choice ⟨any-other-X⟩` downstream. Either use `Classical.choose` on the existential chain (preserves the spec), or `Nonempty.some`-with-witness; or ship a typed `sorry`.
43	- **Empty-content `proof_wanted`.** `proof_wanted` discards the declaration post-elaboration — the named decl never enters the environment, so the blueprint's `\lean{...}` cross-reference breaks. Use `theorem name : T := by sorry` with the intended signature instead.
44	
45	**Litmus test**: if you `unfold` your declaration, does it expose the named substantive content (Kähler differential module, the explicit iso, …) or does it stop at `Classical.choice` / `Iso.refl _` / nothing? If the latter, the body is structurally vacuous — ship the typed `sorry` instead.
46	
47	## When infrastructure is missing
48	
49	Do NOT report "Mathlib lacks X" and stop. Before giving up:
50	
51	1. **Use the informal agent** (`.claude/tools/archon-informal-agent.py`): "Prove [goal] without using [missing infrastructure], only Mathlib." Even an imperfect sketch is valuable.
52	2. **Try the alternative** — formalize whatever the informal agent suggests.
53	3. **If you still can't**: write the alternative sketch to `informal/<theorem_name>.md` and record in your task result what you tried, why it failed, AND the alternative route you found. "I couldn't prove X, but here's approach Y that might work because Z" is far more useful than "infrastructure missing".
54	
55	When stuck more generally: break into smaller subgoals, search Mathlib more thoroughly, prove missing helpers yourself, try alternative strategies, re-read the blueprint, use Web Search for published proofs.
56	
57	**Impossibility vs difficulty**: technical difficulty → keep trying. Mathematical impossibility → immediately backtrack and document why.
58	
59	## Proof style
60	
61	- **Never modify working proofs** — if a declaration has no `sorry` and compiles, do not touch its body.
62	- Keep edits minimal; don't delete comments or change labels; don't add unrelated declarations.
63	- Helper lemmas you introduced may be modified if they turn out wrong.
64	- Add a concise comment above each helper lemma so reuse is easy.
65	- **`change` vs `show`** — `change` reshapes the goal up to defeq; `show` is purely display-level annotation. Using `show` where `change` is needed produces a linter warning. Default to `change` when in doubt.
66	
67	## Mathlib tags in PROGRESS.md
68	
69	The plan agent tags suggested lemmas:
70	
71	- `[verified]` — confirmed to exist.
72	- `[expected]` — guessed by naming convention. Quick `lean_local_search`; pivot if it doesn't exist.
73	- `[gap]` — verified NOT in Mathlib. Don't waste search time; formalize a workaround.
74	
75	## LSP MCP tools
76	
77	The `archon-lean-lsp` server exposes Lean LSP operations as **MCP tool calls** (full names like `mcp__archon-lean-lsp__lean_goal` / `mcp__archon-lean-lsp__lean_diagnostic_messages`). The lean4 skill reference uses short names (`lean_goal`, `lean_local_search`, …) — same tools, NOT shell binaries.
78	
79	- Always invoke through your tool-call interface, the same way as `Read` or `Grep`.
80	- **Never** call `Bash` with `lean_goal …` etc. — there is no such shell command.
81	- First LSP action: `mcp__archon-lean-lsp__lean_diagnostic_messages` on your file. If it returns `success: false` the server is cold; retry once or run `lake build` once via Bash, then retry.
82	
83	## Search protocol
84	
85	Follow `references/lean-lsp-tools-api.md`. Key priority:
86	
87	1. `lean_local_search` first.
88	2. `lean_leansearch` for semantic search — describe the mathematical content, not just the name.
89	3. `lean_loogle` for simple type patterns only.
90	4. Never use shell `find` / `grep` to locate Mathlib theorems.
91	
92	## Tooling traps
93	
94	- **Trust `goals_after`, not just empty diagnostics**: in `lean_multi_attempt`, `diagnostics: []` can be misleading (line-scoping quirks attach errors to the enclosing `by` block). Check that `goals_after` is empty or has advanced.
95	- **Beware `lean_run_code` with imports**: standalone snippets can silently swallow elaboration errors when they contain imports. Rely on the actual file's `lean_diagnostic_messages` for authoritative verification.
96	
97	## Logging
98	
99	Write to `task_results/<your_file>.md` (mirror your `.lean` path: `Algebra/WLocal.lean` → `task_results/Algebra_WLocal.lean.md`):
100	
101	```markdown
102	# Algebra/WLocal.lean
103	
104	## wLocal_iff (line 45)
105	### Attempt 1
106	- **Approach:** Direct case split on maximal ideals
107	- **Result:** FAILED — needed IsLocalRing instance, not available
108	- **Dead end:** direct case split without IsLocalRing
109	
110	### Attempt 2
111	- **Approach:** Stacks 0A31, characterize via bijection on spectra
112	- **Result:** RESOLVED
113	- **Key insight:** `PrimeSpectrum.comap_injective` bridges the gap
114	
115	## helper_bijective (line 78)
116	### Attempt 1
117	- **Approach:** Split into injectivity + surjectivity; prove injectivity
118	- **Result:** PARTIAL (branch closed) — injectivity closed; surjectivity remains
119	- **Next step:** `PrimeSpectrum.range_comap_of_surjective` for the right branch
120	- **Lemmas found:** `PrimeSpectrum.comap_surjective`
121	```
122

9	
10	/-!
11	# The line bundle `𝒪_C(P)` of a closed point and its global sections (RR.3)
12	
13	This file is the **RR.3** sub-build chapter for the project's headline
14	`genusZero_curve_iso_P1` (the "smooth proper geometrically irreducible
15	genus-`0` curve over `k̄` is isomorphic to `ℙ¹`" lemma in
16	`AlgebraicJacobian.AbelianVarietyRigidity`).
17	
18	The Hartshorne IV.1.3.5 chain for the genus-`0` ↦ `ℙ¹` classification routes
19	through:
20	
21	- `RR.1` (`WeilDivisor.lean`): the Weil divisor group `Div(C)` and the
22	  degree map `deg : Div(C) → ℤ`.
23	- `RR.2` (`RRFormula.lean`): the Euler-characteristic identity
24	  `χ(𝒪_C(D)) = deg(D) + 1 − g` and the Riemann–Roch dimension formula
25	  `ℓ(D) = deg(D) + 1` in genus `0`.
26	- **`RR.3` (this file)**: the invertible sheaf `𝒪_C(P)` of a closed point
27	  `P ∈ C`, its `k̄`-vector space of global sections as the
28	  Riemann–Roch space `L([P])`, the `H¹`-vanishing
29	  `H¹(C, 𝒪_C(P)) = 0` on a genus-`0` curve, the dimension formula
30	  `dim_{k̄} H⁰(C, 𝒪_C(P)) = 2`, and the existence of a non-constant
31	  rational function `f ∈ K(C)` with at most a simple pole at `P`.
32	- `RR.4` (`RationalCurveIso.lean`, future): the "two-section
33	  ⇒ `Proj.fromOfGlobalSections` ⇒ `≅ ℙ¹`" classification.
34	
35	## Status (iter-176 Lane K file-skeleton)
36	
37	This file is the **iter-176 Lane K** file-skeleton (a re-dispatch of the
38	iter-175 Lane K casualty that died to the Anthropic session-limit reset
39	window before the file was ever created). Each of the five pinned
40	declarations carries the *intended* substantive type signature (matching
41	the blueprint `\lean{...}` pin in `chapters/RiemannRoch_OCofP.tex`) with a
42	`sorry` body. The bodies are iter-177+ work after the sibling chapters
43	`RR.1`'s `RationalMap.order` body lands (iter-176 Lane D target) and the
44	`RR.2` cohomology-LES API stabilises.
45	
46	The 5 pinned declarations are:
47	
48	1. `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.lineBundleAtClosedPoint` — the invertible
49	   sheaf `𝒪_C(P)` associated to a closed point `P` on a smooth proper
50	   curve `C / k̄`.
51	2. `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.lineBundleAtClosedPoint.globalSections_iff`
52	   — the identification of global sections of `𝒪_C(P)` with the
53	   Riemann–Roch space
54	   `L([P]) = {f ∈ K(C)^× | div(f) + [P] ≥ 0} ∪ {0}`,
55	   expressed as an `Iff`-style characterisation of the order conditions
56	   `ord_Q(f) ≥ 0` for `Q ≠ P` and `ord_P(f) ≥ −1`.
57	3. `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.lineBundleAtClosedPoint.h1_vanishing_genusZero`
58	   — the cohomological vanishing `H¹(C, 𝒪_C(P)) = 0` on a smooth proper
59	   geometrically irreducible curve `C / k̄` with `g(C) = 0`, via the long
60	   exact sequence of the closed-point short exact sequence
61	   `0 → 𝒪_C → 𝒪_C(P) → k(P) → 0`.
62	4. `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.lineBundleAtClosedPoint.dim_eq_two_of_genusZero`
63	   — the dimension formula `dim_{k̄} H⁰(C, 𝒪_C(P)) = 2` in genus `0`,
64	   specialising the χ-identity `RR.2` to `D = [P]` and consuming the
65	   `H¹`-vanishing of pin 3.
66	5. `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.lineBundleAtClosedPoint.exists_nonconstant_genusZero`
67	   — the corollary: a non-constant rational function `f ∈ K(C)` regular
68	   on `C \ {P}` with at most a simple pole at `P`, obtained as a lift of
69	   any non-zero element of the quotient
70	   `H⁰(C, 𝒪_C(P)) / 𝓀̄ · 1`.
71	
72	## Notation reminders
73	
74	The line bundle `𝒪_C(P)` is realised as a
75	`Sheaf (Opens.grothendieckTopology C.left.toTopCat) (ModuleCat.{u} kbar)`,
76	the same `ModuleCat k̄`-flavoured sheaf category used by the project's
77	`Scheme.HModule` cohomology pipeline (cf. `AlgebraicJacobian.Genus`).
78	Its `H⁰` and `H¹` are computed via `Scheme.HModule kbar (·) 0` and
79	`Scheme.HModule kbar (·) 1`, both of which carry a canonical `Module k̄`
80	instance.
81	
82	## References
83

102	/-! ## §1. The line bundle of a closed point on a smooth proper curve
103	
104	For a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve `C / k̄` and a closed
105	point `P ∈ C`, the local ring `𝒪_{C,P}` is a DVR with maximal ideal
106	generated by a uniformiser `f_P`. Hartshorne's construction `ℒ(D)`
107	(II §6 p. 144) applied to the Cartier divisor `[P]` (locally cut out by
108	`f_P` near `P`, by `1` elsewhere) produces the invertible sheaf `𝒪_C(P)`:
109	the sub-`𝒪_C`-module of the function-field constant sheaf `𝒦_C ≅ K(C)`
110	generated locally by `f_P^{-1}` near `P` and by `1` on `C \ {P}`. -/
111	
112	namespace Scheme
113	
114	/-- **The line bundle `𝒪_C(P)` of a closed point `P` on a smooth proper
115	curve `C / k̄`** (Hartshorne II §6, p. 144, Proposition 6.13(a)).
116	
117	The invertible sheaf cut out (in the dual / `𝒦_C`-subsheaf packaging of
118	Hartshorne `ℒ(D)`) by `f_P^{-1}` near `P` and by `1` on the complement,
119	where `f_P ∈ 𝔪_P ∖ 𝔪_P²` is any uniformiser of the DVR `𝒪_{C,P}`. The
120	result is independent of the choice of uniformiser (two uniformisers
121	differ by a unit) and is an invertible `𝒪_C`-module of rank one.
122	
123	The signature returns a `Sheaf (Opens.grothendieckTopology C.left.toTopCat)
124	(ModuleCat.{u} kbar)`: the same `ModuleCat k̄`-flavoured sheaf
125	carrier used by the project's `Scheme.HModule` cohomology pipeline (so
126	that `H⁰` and `H¹` of `𝒪_C(P)` are accessible via
127	`Scheme.HModule kbar (lineBundleAtClosedPoint P hP) 0/1`).
128	
129	iter-177+ body: glue the inverse-of-ideal-sheaf construction. Concretely,
130	build the ideal sheaf `ℐ_P ⊆ 𝒪_C` of the closed point `P` (locally
131	generated by the uniformiser `f_P` near `P`, equal to `𝒪_C` on the
132	complement) via Mathlib's
133	`AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.IdealSheafData.idealOfPoint`, then take its
134	`𝒪_C`-linear dual `ℋom_{𝒪_C}(ℐ_P, 𝒪_C) ≅ ℐ_P⁻¹`, and finally forget
135	the `O_C`-structure into the `ModuleCat k̄`-valued sheaf via the
136	`HModule` adjunction's left adjoint.
137	
138	Blueprint reference: `def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint`
139	(Hartshorne II §6 p. 144 + Proposition 6.13(a); Stacks tag 01X0). -/
140	noncomputable def lineBundleAtClosedPoint
141	    {kbar : Type u} [Field kbar] [IsAlgClosed kbar]
142	    {C : Over (Spec (.of kbar))} [IsProper C.hom]
143	    [SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1 C.hom]
144	    [GeometricallyIrreducible C.hom] [IsIntegral C.left]
145	    (_P : C.left) (_hP : IsClosed ({_P} : Set C.left)) :
146	    Sheaf (Opens.grothendieckTopology C.left.toTopCat)
147	      (ModuleCat.{u} kbar) :=
148	  sorry
149	
150	namespace lineBundleAtClosedPoint
151	
152	variable {kbar : Type u} [Field kbar] [IsAlgClosed kbar]
153	  {C : Over (Spec (.of kbar))} [IsProper C.hom]
154	  [SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1 C.hom]
155	  [GeometricallyIrreducible C.hom] [IsIntegral C.left]

159	/-! ## §2. Global sections as the Riemann–Roch space `L([P])`
160	
161	Hartshorne II §7 Proposition 7.7 identifies the global sections of
162	`ℒ(D_0)` with the rational functions `f ∈ K(X)^×` satisfying
163	`div(f) ≥ −D_0` (plus the zero section). Specialised to `D_0 = [P]` on a
164	curve, the condition `div(f) + [P] ≥ 0` rewrites coordinate-wise as
165	`ord_Q(f) ≥ 0` for every prime divisor `Q ≠ P` and `ord_P(f) ≥ −1`. -/
166	
167	/-- **Global sections of `𝒪_C(P)` as rational functions with controlled
168	pole at `P`** (Hartshorne II §7 Proposition 7.7, p. 157).
169	
170	For a nonzero rational function `f ∈ K(C)^×`, the following are
171	equivalent:
172	
173	* `f` defines a global section of `𝒪_C(P)` (equivalently: there exists
174	  a nonzero `s ∈ H⁰(C, 𝒪_C(P))` whose image under the canonical
175	  inclusion `𝒪_C(P) ↪ 𝒦_C ≅ K(C)` is `f`);
176	* the order conditions hold: `ord_Q(f) ≥ 0` for every prime divisor
177	  `Q ∈ C.PrimeDivisor` whose generic point is not `P`, and
178	  `ord_P(f) ≥ −1` (where the latter is read off the prime divisor
179	  `⟨P, h⟩` with `h : Order.coheight P = 1` the codimension-one witness
180	  automatic for a closed point on a one-dimensional integral scheme).
181	
182	The iff is the substantive content of Hartshorne's Proposition 7.7(b) /
183	its proof, specialised to `D_0 = [P]`.
184	
185	iter-177+ body: unfold `lineBundleAtClosedPoint` as the subsheaf of
186	`𝒦_C` generated locally by `f_P⁻¹` near `P` and by `1` elsewhere, then
187	read off the order conditions at each stalk via the DVR valuation
188	identification.
189	
190	Blueprint reference: `lem:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_globalSections_iff`
191	(Hartshorne II.7 Proposition 7.7(b), p. 157). -/
192	lemma globalSections_iff
193	    [∀ Q : C.left.PrimeDivisor,
194	        Ring.KrullDimLE 1 (C.left.presheaf.stalk Q.point)]
195	    (P : C.left) (hP : IsClosed ({P} : Set C.left))
196	    (f : C.left.functionField) (_hf : f ≠ 0)
197	    (hPcoh : Order.coheight P = 1) :
198	    (∀ Q : C.left.PrimeDivisor, Q.point ≠ P →
199	        0 ≤ Scheme.RationalMap.order Q f) ∧
200	      (-1 : ℤ) ≤ Scheme.RationalMap.order ⟨P, hPcoh⟩ f
201	    ↔
202	    Nonempty
203	      { s : Scheme.HModule kbar (lineBundleAtClosedPoint (C := C) P hP) 0 //
204	        s ≠ 0 } := by
205	  sorry
206	
207	/-! ## §3. Cohomological vanishing in genus zero
208	
209	Specialise Hartshorne IV.1 Theorem 1.3's inductive step at `D = 0`. The
210	standard short exact sequence
211	`0 → 𝒪_C(−[P]) → 𝒪_C → k(P) → 0` (Hartshorne II.6.18: the ideal sheaf
212	of the locally principal closed subscheme `P` is `𝒪_C(−[P])`; the
213	quotient is the skyscraper `k(P) ≅ k̄` at `P`) tensored by the locally
214	free rank-`1` sheaf `𝒪_C([P])` (left rigid, so preserves exactness and
215	leaves the skyscraper invariant) becomes
216	`0 → 𝒪_C → 𝒪_C(P) → k(P) → 0` in `Coh(C)`. The associated long exact
217	sequence of sheaf cohomology, combined with `H¹(C, 𝒪_C) = 0`
218	(genus-`0` hypothesis: `g(C) = dim_{k̄} H¹(C, 𝒪_C)`) and
219	`H¹(C, k(P)) = 0` (skyscraper / flasque), kills `H¹(C, 𝒪_C(P))`. -/
220	
221	/-- **Vanishing of `H¹(C, 𝒪_C(P))` on a smooth proper geometrically
222	irreducible curve of genus `0`** (Hartshorne IV §1 p. 296, the
223	inductive step of Theorem 1.3 specialised to `D = 0`).
224	
225	Concretely, the finite-dimensional `k̄`-vector space
226	`Scheme.HModule kbar (lineBundleAtClosedPoint P hP) 1` has dimension
227	`0`, i.e. is the trivial vector space.
228	
229	iter-177+ body: assemble the closed-point short exact sequence
230	`0 → 𝒪_C → 𝒪_C(P) → k(P) → 0`, feed it to the long exact sequence of
231	`Module k̄`-flavoured cohomology (the project's
232	`Scheme.HModule k̄`-bridge inherits the LES by forget-functor
233	naturality from
234	`CategoryTheory.Abelian.Ext.covariantSequence_exact`), substitute
235	`H¹(C, 𝒪_C) = 0` (the genus-`0` hypothesis, unfolding
236	`AlgebraicGeometry.genus`) and `H¹(C, k(P)) = 0` (skyscraper sheaf /
237	flasque cohomology, Hartshorne III.2.5), and collapse the segment to
238	`0 → H¹(C, 𝒪_C(P)) → 0`.
239	
240	Blueprint reference: `lem:H1_vanishing_lineBundleAtClosedPoint_genusZero`
241	(Hartshorne IV.1 p. 296). -/
242	lemma h1_vanishing_genusZero
243	    (P : C.left) (hP : IsClosed ({P} : Set C.left))
244	    (_hg : AlgebraicGeometry.genus C = 0) :
245	    Module.finrank kbar
246	        (Scheme.HModule kbar (lineBundleAtClosedPoint (C := C) P hP) 1) = 0 := by
247	  sorry
248	
249	/-! ## §4. The dimension formula `dim H⁰(C, 𝒪_C(P)) = 2` in genus zero
250	
251	Specialise the Euler-characteristic identity
252	`χ(𝒪_C(D)) = deg(D) + 1 − g` of `RR.2`
253	(`Scheme.eulerCharacteristic_eq_degree_plus_one_minus_genus`) to
254	`D = [P]`. Since `deg([P]) = 1` (every closed point contributes degree
255	`1` over `k̄`) and `g(C) = 0`, this gives `χ(𝒪_C(P)) = 2`. Unfolding
256	`χ` as `dim H⁰ − dim H¹` and substituting the `H¹`-vanishing of §3
257	yields `dim H⁰(C, 𝒪_C(P)) = 2`. -/
258	
259	/-- **The dimension formula `dim_{k̄} H⁰(C, 𝒪_C(P)) = 2` on a smooth
260	proper geometrically irreducible genus-`0` curve over `k̄`**
261	(Hartshorne IV §1 Example 1.3.5, p. 297).
262	
263	iter-177+ body: invoke
264	`Scheme.eulerCharacteristic_eq_degree_plus_one_minus_genus` on the
265	`ModuleCat k̄`-valued sheaf `lineBundleAtClosedPoint P hP` (matching the
266	χ-identity through a bridge identifying
267	`lineBundleAtClosedPoint P hP` with
268	`WeilDivisor.sheafOf (ofClosedPoint P hP)`), evaluate the right-hand
269	side `deg([P]) + 1 − g(C) = 1 + 1 − 0 = 2`, unfold
270	`Scheme.eulerCharacteristic` as
271	`(Module.finrank kbar H⁰) − (Module.finrank kbar H¹)`, substitute
272	`Module.finrank kbar H¹ = 0` from `h1_vanishing_genusZero`, and read
273	off `Module.finrank kbar H⁰ = 2`.
274	
275	Blueprint reference: `thm:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_dim_eq_two_of_genusZero`
276	(Hartshorne IV.1 Example 1.3.5, p. 297). -/
277	theorem dim_eq_two_of_genusZero
278	    (P : C.left) (hP : IsClosed ({P} : Set C.left))
279	    (_hg : AlgebraicGeometry.genus C = 0) :
280	    Module.finrank kbar
281	        (Scheme.HModule kbar (lineBundleAtClosedPoint (C := C) P hP) 0) = 2 := by
282	  sorry
283	
284	/-! ## §5. A non-constant rational function with at most a simple pole at `P`
285	
286	The two-dimensionality of `H⁰(C, 𝒪_C(P))` and the one-dimensional
287	constant subspace `k̄ · 1` give a non-zero quotient `H⁰/k̄`. Any lift
288	of a non-zero element of the quotient is, under the identification of
289	`globalSections_iff`, a non-constant rational function `f ∈ K(C)` with
290	the order conditions `ord_Q(f) ≥ 0` for `Q ≠ P` and `ord_P(f) ≥ −1`.
291	This is the seed of `RR.4` (the morphism `C → ℙ¹` produced by
292	`Proj.fromOfGlobalSections` from the basis `(1, f)`). -/
293	
294	/-- **Existence of a non-constant rational function regular on `C ∖ {P}`
295	with at most a simple pole at `P`** (Hartshorne IV §1 Exercise 1.1,
296	p. 297, the genus-`0` specialisation).
297	
298	Concretely, there exists `f ∈ K(C)` such that:
299	
300	* `f ≠ 0`;
301	* `f ∉ k̄` (i.e. `f` is non-constant — for instance, it does not lie
302	  in the image of the structural inclusion of constants);
303	* `ord_Q(f) ≥ 0` for every prime divisor `Q ∈ C.PrimeDivisor` whose
304	  generic point is not `P`;
305	* `ord_P(f) ≥ −1` (at most a simple pole at `P`).
306	
307	iter-177+ body: use `dim_eq_two_of_genusZero` to get
308	`dim_{k̄} H⁰(C, 𝒪_C(P)) = 2`. The image of `1 ∈ H⁰(C, 𝒪_C) ≅ k̄`
309	under the structural inclusion `𝒪_C ↪ 𝒪_C(P)` spans a one-dimensional
310	subspace of `H⁰(C, 𝒪_C(P))`; choose any element of the complement
311	(non-empty because `dim H⁰ = 2 > 1`), lift it through
312	`globalSections_iff` to a rational function `f`, and verify that the
313	three bullets hold. The lift is non-constant because it is not in the
314	constant subspace.
315	
316	The principal-divisor-non-zero formulation `Scheme.WeilDivisor.principal
317	f hf ≠ 0` follows from non-constancy plus the fact that constant
318	functions have principal divisor zero (the converse — `div(f) = 0`
319	⇒ `f` constant — uses the integrality of `C` and is the Stacks 02P0
320	"functions with no zeros and poles are constant" type statement).
321	
322	Blueprint reference:
323	`cor:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_exists_nonconstant_genusZero` (alias
324	`cor:nonconstant_function_genus_zero` consumed by `RR.4`)
325	(Hartshorne IV.1 Exercise 1.1, p. 297). -/
326	theorem exists_nonconstant_genusZero
327	    [∀ Q : C.left.PrimeDivisor,
328	        Ring.KrullDimLE 1 (C.left.presheaf.stalk Q.point)]
329	    (P : C.left) (_hP : IsClosed ({P} : Set C.left))
330	    (hPcoh : Order.coheight P = 1)
331	    (_hg : AlgebraicGeometry.genus C = 0) :
332	    ∃ (f : C.left.functionField) (hf : f ≠ 0),
333	      (∀ Q : C.left.PrimeDivisor, Q.point ≠ P →
334	          0 ≤ Scheme.RationalMap.order Q f) ∧
335	      (-1 : ℤ) ≤ Scheme.RationalMap.order ⟨P, hPcoh⟩ f ∧
336	      Scheme.WeilDivisor.principal (X := C.left) f hf ≠ 0 := by
337	  sorry
338	
339	end lineBundleAtClosedPoint
340	
341	end Scheme
342	
343	end AlgebraicGeometry
